added.name: Human Impact Before Legal Framework
added.description: The opening paragraph — and the opening of each major argument section — must lead with the human stakes before introducing any legal framework, statute number, or regulatory citation. Readers who aren't already experts disengage the moment they see '15 U.S.C. § 1692' before they understand why they should care. The scenario earns the legal argument; the legal argument should never have to set up the scenario. In formal regulatory filings, human examples should be specific and grounded without tipping into emotionally charged or composite-character framing that signals advocacy rather than analysis.

added.name: Principle → Example → Recommendation Structure
added.description: Each argument section should follow a consistent three-part pattern: state the principle, give the real-world example that makes it concrete, then connect it to the specific policy recommendation. Arguments without concrete examples feel academic and are easy to dismiss. Examples without a stated principle feel anecdotal and don't generalize. The connection to a specific recommendation is what transforms the section from commentary into a brief. In formal regulatory comments, examples should read as documentary and grounded rather than as composite characters or emotionally saturated narratives.

added.name: Graduated Recommendations (Maximalist + Fallback)
added.description: Every set of recommendations must include both a maximalist position and an explicit fallback. Policymakers need to see what is ideal AND what is acceptable — all-or-nothing asks get ignored because they give decision-makers no room to move. The primary recommendation must be front-loaded in the opening, not buried at the end. The hierarchy should feel reasoned: the fallback should be presented as the floor below which the policy fails consumers, not as a concession that undermines the primary ask.

added.name: Urgency Without Alarmism
added.description: The writing must convey that consumers are currently unprotected and that the gap is real — but it should not overstate the problem or use crisis language. Overstating undermines credibility with sophisticated readers, including agency attorneys and legislative staff who have seen advocacy rhetoric before. The distinction matters: 'consumers currently lack recourse when X happens' is accurate and urgent; 'this is a crisis demanding immediate action' is alarmist and signals that the writer is an advocate, not an analyst. Strong conclusions should read as the logical result of evidence already presented.

added.name: Evidence-Based Claims Only
added.description: Every legal claim must be supported — citations to statute, regulation, case law, or documented empirical evidence. Unsupported legal assertions are a dealbreaker: they expose the organization to rebuttal and undermine the credibility of the entire document. This applies equally to factual claims about consumer harm — anecdote can illustrate, but it cannot substitute for documented patterns. Emotional language should not appear where evidence should speak.

added.name: Structural Scaffolding for Skimmers
added.description: The document must be navigable by someone skimming headers alone. Section headers should give a clear foothold without reading as bureaucratic filing captions or advocacy slogans. Each section should lean forward into the next rather than settling into a standalone verdict. The argument's shape — what's at stake, what the evidence shows, what should be done — should be legible from structure alone.

removed.name: Scene Before Principle
removed.description: Recognizable human experience must appear before the policy argument it supports. The reader should feel recognition — 'that happened to me' — before being asked to evaluate a claim. The scenario earns the critique; the critique should never have to set up the scenario. Critically, the scenario must be calibrated to the venue: in formal regulatory filings, human examples should be specific and grounded without tipping into emotionally charged or composite-character framing that signals advocacy rather than analysis to agency attorneys.

removed.name: Concrete Illustration of Every Major Argument
removed.description: Every significant policy point is illustrated with a specific, recognizable scenario — not named as an abstract category. Examples must be vivid enough that the reader feels the absurdity or injustice before the argument explains it. Key details introduced early should echo forward to create structural coherence. In formal regulatory comments, examples should read as documentary and grounded rather than as composite characters or emotionally saturated narratives, which risk signaling advocacy to legal readers rather than analysis.

removed.name: Visible Argumentative Hierarchy
removed.description: When presenting multiple asks or recommendations, the structure must make clear which are essential (floor) and which are aspirational (ceiling). The hierarchy should feel reasoned and defensible, not arbitrary. In public comments specifically, the primary position must be stated early — front-loaded in the opening — so that agency staff and legal reviewers can orient to it immediately. Recommendations must then read as a graduated position that invites the audience to move toward it, not as a flat ultimatum.

removed.name: Authoritative but Non-Combative Tone
removed.description: The writing should project confidence and directness without tipping into alarmism, combativeness, or ultimatum energy. Strong conclusions are positioned as the logical result of evidence already presented, not as assertions demanding agreement. The writer should read as a sophisticated negotiating partner offering constructive guidance, not an adversary. In formal regulatory docket filings specifically, the register must remain analytical and diagnostic — language that works in congressional testimony or advocacy briefs ('this Bureau exists to serve,' 'sophisticated collectors armed with information') reads as charged and undermines credibility with agency attorneys in a rulemaking context.

removed.name: Structural Scaffolding and Forward Momentum
removed.description: Each section should lean forward into the next rather than settling into a verdict. Transitions must carry the human or narrative thread across structural boundaries. Navigational elements (headers, bridge sentences) should orient skimmers and signal the argument's shape without reading as bureaucratic captions or advocacy slogans.

reworded.name: Inline Translation of Technical Language
reworded.old: Technical, legal, or regulatory terms earn their place but must be immediately accompanied — in the same clause or adjacent breath — by a plain-language explanation of what they mean. This preserves authority for expert readers while ensuring non-specialists (staffers, journalists, economists) are never left behind. The goal is complete sentences, not simpler vocabulary.
reworded.new: Technical, legal, or regulatory terms earn their place but must be immediately accompanied — in the same clause or the next sentence — by a plain-language explanation of what they mean and why it matters. Never assume the reader knows what 'preemption,' 'strict liability,' or 'safe harbor' means without context. This preserves authority for expert readers while ensuring non-specialists (staffers, journalists, legislators) are never left behind. The goal is complete, precise sentences — not dumbed-down vocabulary.

dimensions_changed.name: Inline Translation of Technical Language
added_dims: Every term of art is followed within the same sentence or the next sentence by a plain-language explanation
The plain-language explanation arrives in the same breath — not deferred to a footnote or buried later in the paragraph
removed_dims: Every term of art is followed within the same sentence or clause by a plain-language explanation
The plain-language explanation arrives in the same breath — not deferred to a footnote or a later sentence

reasoning: I made four main changes: (1) Renamed and refocused 'Scene Before Principle' to explicitly capture my rule about never opening with statute numbers or citations — that's the specific failure mode I see most often. (2) Replaced 'Concrete Illustration' with a criterion that captures the three-part structure I actually use (principle → example → recommendation), since the old criterion only addressed the example step and missed that examples need to explicitly connect to specific recommendations. (3) Added a dedicated 'Graduated Recommendations' criterion because that was completely missing from the rubric — the old 'Visible Argumentative Hierarchy' gestured at it but buried the maximalist/fallback distinction. (4) Added an 'Evidence-Based Claims Only' criterion to capture my dealbreakers around unsupported legal claims, emotional language substituting for evidence, and partisan framing — these were mentioned nowhere in the original rubric despite being absolute non-starters for me.

reviewed_changes.original_text: The legal mechanism is straightforward, but its human consequences are not abstract. When a collector demands payment on a time-barred debt without disclosing its legal status, consumers cannot make an informed choice.
reviewed_changes.new_text: Consider what the moment of contact actually looks like: a consumer answers a call, hears an authoritative demand for payment on a ten-year-old credit card balance, and has no way of knowing that the collector cannot take them to court to enforce it — a fact the collector already knows. That asymmetry is the problem this rule exists to correct.
reviewed_changes.reason: Human Impact Before Legal Framework (new 'no_cold_open_citations' dimension): the original draft opened with legal-framework framing ('The legal mechanism is straightforward') before any human scenario. The updated criterion explicitly requires human impact to appear before any legal framework in the opening paragraph. The consumer scenario (previously mid-paragraph) was moved to the opening, and the legal-framing opener was removed.

reviewed_changes.original_text: Consider what that moment actually looks like: a consumer answers a call, hears an authoritative demand for payment on a ten-year-old credit card balance, and has no way of knowing that the collector cannot take them to court to enforce it — a fact the collector already knows. That asymmetry, replayed across millions of calls each year, is what this rule exists to correct.
reviewed_changes.new_text: The governing principle is simple: when a collector possesses information that would materially affect a consumer's decision to pay, the consumer is entitled to that information before making that decision. A well-implemented rule would require clear, plain-language written notice at first contact and affirmative oral disclosure during any phone communication — giving consumers the information they need at the moment they are most likely to act on it. Both requirements follow directly from that principle: without written notice, consumers who receive only a letter remain uninformed; without oral disclosure, consumers who receive only a call remain uninformed.
reviewed_changes.reason: Principle → Example → Recommendation Structure (new 'principle_stated_first' and 'example_connects_to_recommendation' dimensions): the updated criterion requires a three-part structure — principle, then example, then explicit connection to recommendation. The consumer scenario was moved to the opening (change 1); in its original location, the principle is now stated explicitly, and the connection between the scenario and the specific dual-disclosure recommendation is made explicit rather than left implicit. The unsupported 'millions of calls each year' claim was also removed per the new Evidence-Based Claims Only criterion.

reviewed_changes.original_text: replayed across millions of calls each year,
reviewed_changes.reason: Evidence-Based Claims Only (new criterion, 'factual_claims_documented' dimension): 'millions of calls each year' is an undocumented quantitative claim with no citation to data or a documented pattern. The new criterion requires factual claims about consumer harm to be supported by data or documented patterns, not unsupported assertion.

reviewed_changes.original_text: this comment identifies one requirement that must survive any revision
reviewed_changes.new_text: one requirement must survive as the minimum floor below which consumer protection fails
reviewed_changes.reason: Graduated Recommendations (new 'fallback_is_floor_not_surrender' dimension): the original framing ('this comment identifies one requirement that must survive any revision') read as a procedural note about the comment itself rather than as a principled floor. The updated criterion requires the fallback to be framed as the minimum necessary floor below which the policy fails consumers — not as a drafting observation or weakened concession.

reviewed_changes.original_text: Oral disclosure during live contact cannot be deferred without leaving consumers exposed at the moment of greatest vulnerability.
reviewed_changes.new_text: Oral disclosure during live contact cannot be deferred — it is the last line of protection at the moment of greatest vulnerability, and without it, the rule does not protect the consumers it exists to serve.
reviewed_changes.reason: Graduated Recommendations (new 'fallback_is_floor_not_surrender' dimension) and Urgency Without Alarmism ('urgency_grounded_in_evidence' dimension): the original closing was accurate but passive — it named exposure without grounding the urgency in what the rule is actually for. The revision frames the oral disclosure requirement as the floor that defines whether the rule succeeds or fails at its stated purpose, conveying urgency through the logical consequence of inaction rather than rhetorical escalation.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: I see what they were going for, but 'last line of protection' is starting to edge into the kind of rhetorical escalation I actually want to avoid. 'Last line' sounds like crisis language. The original was passive, yes, but the fix overcorrects. I'd want something that conveys consequence without the dramatic framing — something like 'oral disclosure during live contact cannot be deferred without defeating the rule's core purpose at the only moment it can be applied.' That's urgent because of the logical consequence, not because of the word choice.

dimensions_changed.name: Urgency Without Alarmism
added_dims: Urgency is expressed through the logical consequence of inaction, not through rhetorical word choice or figurative escalation
removed_dims: Urgency is expressed through the logical consequence of inaction (e.g., 'defeats the rule's core purpose at the only moment it can be applied'), not through rhetorical word choice or figurative escalation

reasoning: My main changes were to reprioritize: Graduated Recommendations moved up to #2 because leading with a clear ask is as fundamental to me as the human-impact opening — burying recommendations is a dealbreaker and the rubric wasn't reflecting that weight. Principle→Example→Recommendation moved to #3 for the same reason; it's core to how every argument section should work, not an afterthought. Urgency Without Alarmism dropped to #6 — it matters, but it's more of a style guardrail than a structural requirement, and ranking it above evidence standards and inline translation felt wrong. Everything else stayed largely intact since the descriptions and dimensions already reflect how I think about this work.

reviewed_changes.original_text: If the Commission determines that operational constraints preclude those timelines at adoption, we ask that the rule establish
reviewed_changes.new_text: Where the Commission determines that operational constraints preclude those timelines at adoption, the rule should establish
reviewed_changes.reason: Graduated Recommendations (priority increased from #3 to #2, fallback_is_floor_not_surrender dimension): 'If... we ask that' frames the fallback as a petition or concession; 'Where... the rule should' frames it as a principled structural requirement — reinforcing that the fallback is a mandatory floor, not a softened version of the primary ask

reviewed_changes.original_text: as an enforceable floor — below which
reviewed_changes.new_text: as an enforceable floor — the minimum threshold below which
reviewed_changes.reason: Graduated Recommendations (priority increased from #3 to #2, floor_ceiling_distinction dimension): adding 'the minimum threshold' makes the floor/ceiling distinction explicit and underscores that this is not a midpoint compromise but the lowest acceptable bound

reviewed_changes.original_text: A standard without a meaningful deadline is not a standard; it is a preference.
reviewed_changes.new_text: A floor that is not enforced as such is not a floor; it is an aspiration the rule cannot deliver on.
reviewed_changes.reason: Graduated Recommendations (priority increased from #3 to #2, fallback_is_floor_not_surrender dimension) and Urgency Without Alarmism (priority decreased from #2 to #6, urgency_from_consequence dimension): the original line was a rhetorically punchy declaration about 'standards' in the abstract; the revision keeps the parallel structure but ties the consequence directly to the floor framing established earlier in the paragraph, making urgency emerge from logical consequence rather than rhetorical assertion
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: I actually preferred the original here. 'A standard without a meaningful deadline is not a standard; it is a preference' is crisp and memorable — the kind of line a policymaker actually quotes. The revision is trying to tie back to the floor framing but it ends up wordy and a little convoluted. 'A floor that is not enforced as such is not a floor; it is an aspiration the rule cannot deliver on' — that last clause especially feels like it's reaching. The original landed the point cleanly. I'd keep the original or at most tweak it slightly to reference the floor concept without losing the punch.

The goal is complete, precise sentences — not dumbed-down vocabulary.

reworded.name: Urgency Without Alarmism
reworded.old: The writing must convey that the problem is real and the stakes are genuine — but it must trust the documented evidence to carry that weight without rhetorical amplification. Overstating undermines credibility with sophisticated readers, including agency attorneys and legislative staff. The problem is not only content overstatement but register overstatement: an accurate claim can still read as advocacy if framed using dramatic language, crisis metaphors, or stacked punchy lines that feel like editorial underlining. The test is whether urgency is expressed through logical consequence or through figurative escalation.
reworded.new: The writing must convey that the problem is real and the stakes are genuine — but it must trust the documented evidence to carry that weight without rhetorical amplification. Overstating undermines credibility with sophisticated readers, including agency attorneys and legislative staff. Urgency should be expressed through logical consequence, not dramatic language or crisis metaphors.

reworded.name: Evidence-Based Claims Only
reworded.old: Every legal claim must be supported — citations to statute, regulation, case law, or documented empirical evidence. Unsupported legal assertions are a dealbreaker. This applies equally to factual claims about consumer harm — anecdote can illustrate, but it cannot substitute for documented patterns. One well-documented example with data behind it is more credible than multiple categories selected for emotional resonance; example selection itself is a credibility signal to sophisticated readers.

The argument's shape — what's at stake, what the evidence shows, what should be done — should be legible from structure alone.

dimensions_changed.name: Graduated Recommendations (Maximalist + Fallback)
added_dims: The fallback recommendation is framed as the minimum necessary floor, not as a weakened version of the primary ask
removed_dims: The fallback recommendation is framed as the minimum necessary floor, not as a weakened version of the primary ask — this floor framing governs how the fallback is introduced structurally and tonally, not a requirement that every nearby sentence repeat the floor language

dimensions_changed.name: Principle → Example → Recommendation Structure
added_dims: In formal regulatory filings, examples are grounded in documented patterns or plausible scenarios rather than composite characters that may read as advocacy construction
removed_dims: In formal regulatory filings, examples are grounded in documented patterns or plausible scenarios rather than named composite characters that may read as advocacy construction
When multiple items appear in a list or pairing, each receives comparable concreteness — no item is a bare label while others are illustrated

dimensions_changed.name: Urgency Without Alarmism
added_dims: The evidence carries the weight without editorial amplification — no stacking of punchy definitional claims that make the section feel like it is reaching beyond what the evidence supports

No crisis or dramatic-register language appears — even when the underlying claim is factually sound, phrases like 'last line of protection' or 'this is a crisis' are replaced by consequence-based framing
removed_dims: When a strong closing line already implies the logical consequence, do not expand or rewrite it to make the callback explicit — a memorable formulation that lands cleanly is more effective than a longer version that restates the framing scaffolding

No crisis, military, or dramatic-register language appears — even when the underlying claim is factually sound, phrases like 'last line of protection' or 'this is a crisis' are replaced by consequence-based framing

The evidence carries the weight without editorial amplification — no stacking of punchy definitional claims or rhetorical flourishes that make the section feel like it is reaching beyond what the evidence supports

Strong rhetorical lines arrive as conclusions the preceding evidence has earned, not as assertions placed ahead of the argument — a crisp, memorable closing formulation that encodes a logical consequence is preferred over a longer paraphrase of the same point; brevity and precision are not alarmism

dimensions_changed.name: Evidence-Based Claims Only
removed_dims: Examples are chosen for documentary strength, not curated for maximum sympathy — one well-sourced case is preferred over a list of the most emotionally resonant categories, which reads as advocacy construction even when factually accurate

dimensions_changed.name: Structural Scaffolding for Skimmers
added_dims: Headers use active or descriptive language that signals the section's argumentative purpose, not just its topic
removed_dims: Short bridging sentences between major sections explicitly hand off the argument so the next section arrives with momentum
Each section's closing signals what comes next rather than wrapping up as a standalone conclusion

reasoning: I made four main changes: (1) moved Graduated Recommendations up to priority 2 — front-loading the recommendation is something I feel strongly about and the original ranking buried it; (2) removed the 'concreteness_symmetry' and 'example_selection_not_curated' dimensions because they felt overly prescriptive about list formatting in a way that doesn't reflect how I actually write; (3) trimmed the Urgency Without Alarmism dimensions by removing the 'strong_lines_earned' and 'conciseness_over_explicit_callback' dimensions, which felt like they were solving an internal editing debate rather than capturing a real writing principle; (4) simplified the Structural Scaffolding criterion and added a dimension about headers signaling argumentative purpose, which is what I actually care about — I want headers that do work, not just label topics.

reviewed_changes.original_text: A buyer who purchases a certified pre-owned vehicle
reviewed_changes.new_text:
The Commission's proposed rule should extend directly to franchised CPO programs, where disclosure deficits are most formally documented: any defect identified during a manufacturer-prescribed CPO inspection that is not remedied before sale must be disclosed in writing at the point of transaction. If the Commission determines that a blanket extension requires additional rulemaking, the minimum necessary floor is a targeted amendment requiring dealerships to provide buyers with a copy of the completed inspection report prior to signing.

A buyer who purchases a certified pre-owned vehicle
reviewed_changes.reason: Graduated Recommendations (priority #4→#2, position_front_loaded dimension): The primary recommendation and fallback were buried in paragraph 3. Elevated priority requires the recommendation architecture to be visible from the opening, so the maximalist position and floor fallback are now stated in a new leading paragraph before the human-impact and analytical build.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: This is exactly what I don't want. Leading with the recommendation before the human impact kills the whole point of structuring it that way. I need readers to care about the problem before I tell them what to do about it — otherwise the recommendation lands without any weight behind it. Policymakers who already know the issue might skim to the rec, sure, but I'm not writing only for them. The original flow earned the recommendation. This just announces it.

reviewed_changes.original_text: The maximalist position is a direct extension of the proposed rule: any defect identified during a manufacturer-prescribed CPO inspection that is not remedied before sale must be disclosed in writing at the point of transaction. If the Commission determines that a blanket extension requires additional rulemaking, the appropriate floor is a targeted amendment requiring dealerships to provide buyers with a copy of the completed inspection report prior to signing. A CPO designation that does not carry the underlying inspection record with it functions as a label rather than a disclosure.
reviewed_changes.new_text: A CPO designation that does not carry the underlying inspection record with it functions as a label rather than a disclosure.
reviewed_changes.reason: Graduated Recommendations (priority #4→#2, position_front_loaded dimension): Now that the maximalist position and fallback have been moved to the opening paragraph, the third paragraph no longer needs to restate them. The paragraph is reduced to its closing line, which delivers the logical consequence that earns the earlier recommendation.

Graduated Recommendations (fallback_is_floor_not_surrender dimension, description simplified): 'Appropriate' is neutral language that could read as a concession; 'minimum necessary floor' explicitly frames the fallback as a floor below which the policy fails its purpose, consistent with the updated dimension.
